'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.

== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.

Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).

The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

[[uk:РЎРәСғРҝСҮРөРҪРҪСҸ РәСғСҖРіР°РҪС–РІ РһСҸРјР°СӮРҫ]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[Category:з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[ja:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

[[uk:РЎРәСғРҝСҮРөРҪРҪСҸ РәСғСҖРіР°РҪС–РІ РһСҸРјР°СӮРҫ]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[Category:з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[ja:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

[[uk:РЎРәСғРҝСҮРөРҪРҪСҸ РәСғСҖРіР°РҪС–РІ РһСҸРјР°СӮРҫ]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[Category:з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[ja:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

[[uk:РЎРәСғРҝСҮРөРҪРҪСҸ РәСғСҖРіР°РҪС–РІ РһСҸРјР°СӮРҫ]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[Category:з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[ja:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

[[uk:РЎРәСғРҝСҮРөРҪРҪСҸ РәСғСҖРіР°РҪС–РІ РһСҸРјР°СӮРҫ]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:long o change]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-qq]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Oyamato Kofun Group}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[Category:з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]][[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]][[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[ja:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]]

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.
