{{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:History of Tenri City]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kofun clusters]] [[Category:National Historic Sites in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[Category:еҶҶеўі]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }}
[[sn:ЕҢyamato Kofun Group]] [[ja:еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ]] [[uk:РЎРәСғРҝСҮРөРҪРҪСҸ РәСғСҖРіР°РҪС–РІ РһСҸРјР°СӮРҫ]]
<!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-->

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.

The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, classified as a "great king tomb." <!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group--><!--ЕҢyamato Kofun Group-->

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.

This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).

== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

revision.text:
'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenri, Nara Prefecture|qq=}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Sites of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Basin|qq=}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|qq=}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢyamato Shrine|qq=}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor KeikЕҚ|qq=}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoikeyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Tashiraka|qq=}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd century|qq=}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/4th century|qq=}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special pedestal-shaped vessels|qq=}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanukite|qq=}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

revision.text:
'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenri, Nara Prefecture|qq=}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Sites of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Basin|qq=}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|qq=}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢyamato Shrine|qq=}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor KeikЕҚ|qq=}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoikeyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Tashiraka|qq=}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd century|qq=}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/4th century|qq=}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special pedestal-shaped vessels|qq=}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanukite|qq=}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|" to "|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|lt=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|1=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|"
revision.text:
'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenri, Nara Prefecture|qq=}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Sites of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Basin|qq=}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|qq=}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢyamato Shrine|qq=}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|lt=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|1=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor KeikЕҚ|qq=}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoikeyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Tashiraka|qq=}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd century|qq=}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/4th century|qq=}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special pedestal-shaped vessels|qq=}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanukite|qq=}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Makimuku Kofun Group]] вҶ’ [[Makimuku Kofun Cluster]]
revision.text:
'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenri, Nara Prefecture|qq=}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Sites of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Basin|qq=}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|qq=}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢyamato Shrine|qq=}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|lt=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|1=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor KeikЕҚ|qq=}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoikeyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Tashiraka|qq=}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd century|qq=}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/4th century|qq=}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special pedestal-shaped vessels|qq=}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanukite|qq=}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Yanagimoto Kofun Group]] вҶ’ [[Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster]]
revision.text:
'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenri, Nara Prefecture|qq=}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Sites of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Basin|qq=}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|qq=}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢyamato Shrine|qq=}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|lt=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|1=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor KeikЕҚ|qq=}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoikeyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Tashiraka|qq=}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd century|qq=}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/4th century|qq=}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special pedestal-shaped vessels|qq=}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanukite|qq=}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.

'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Group''' (еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ) is a [[Kofun Group]] located in the southern part of {{ill|Tenri, Nara Prefecture|ja|еӨ©зҗҶеёӮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenri, Nara Prefecture|qq=}} (иҗұз”ҹз”ә to дёӯеұұз”ә). Some parts of the group have been designated as {{ill|Historic Sites of Japan|ja|еҸІи·Ў|1=Historic site|lt=Historic site of Japan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Sites of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic site|qq=}}}.
It is also referred to as "'''ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster'''." The largest kofun is {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}. Along the southeastern foothills of the {{ill|Nara Basin|ja|еҘҲиүҜзӣҶең°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Basin|qq=}}, three Kofun GroupsвҖ”{{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, and ЕҢyamato Kofun GroupвҖ”are located in a north-south alignment along the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|ja|еҲқзҖ¬е·қ (еҘҲиүҜзңҢ)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara Prefecture)|qq=}}. Although further divided into three smaller groups, they can collectively be referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Cluster" in its broadest sense.
Excluding the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qq=}} (also known as Hashimoto Kofun Group), which contains early {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} such as {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|з®ёеў“еҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}}, the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group (in the narrower sense) and the Yanagimoto Kofun Group are considered to have been constructed slightly later. Together, they are sometimes called the '''ЕҢyamato-Yanagimoto Kofun Group'''. These kofun are believed to have been created during the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The group is referred to as the "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group" due to the presence of the {{ill|ЕҢyamato Shrine|ja|еӨ§е’ҢзҘһзӨҫ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ЕҢyamato Shrine|qq=}} at the western edge of the kofun cluster. Within the group, there are 12 known {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢеҶҶеўі|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}, 5 {{ill|ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|ja|еүҚж–№еҫҢж–№еўі|lt=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|1=ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ZenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpЕҚ-kЕҚhЕҚ-fun|qq=}}, and 7 {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|еҶҶеўі|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=}}. These tombs can be further divided into two sub-groups: one on the hills (Nakayama subgroup) and one on the alluvial fan (KayЕҚ subgroup). Accordingly, the names "Nakayama Kofun Group" and "KayЕҚ Kofun Group" are often used.
The ЕҢyamato Kofun Group lacks significant size differences among the tombs, distinguishing it from the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Cluster|lt=Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|жҹіжң¬еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagimoto Kofun Group|qq=}}, which emphasizes primary tombs and subsidiary tombs, and the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster|lt=Makimuku Kofun Group|ja|зәҸеҗ‘еҸӨеўізҫӨ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makimuku Kofun Group|qq=}}, which also features a primary-subsidiary tomb relationship. Notably, subsidiary tombs do not involve forced burial practices.
The largest keyhole-shaped kofun within the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group, the {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}} (mound length of 234 meters), is thought to have been constructed earlier than the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|иЎҢзҮҲеұұеҸӨеўі|1=Andon-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} (241 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|еҙҮзҘһеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|ja|жёӢи°·еҗ‘еұұеҸӨеўі|1=Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun|qq=}} (310 meters, currently designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor KeikЕҚ|ja|жҷҜиЎҢеӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor KeikЕҚ|qq=}}) in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group.
Large tombs believed to be royal burial sites moved to the northwestern part of the Nara Basin by the latter half of the early Kofun period, forming the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|дҪҗзҙҖзӣҫеҲ—еҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}.
In {{ill|2014|ja|е№іжҲҗ26е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2014|qq=}} (October 6), the burial areas of the Nomugi Kofun, {{ill|Shimoikeyama Kofun|ja|дёӢжұ еұұеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoikeyama Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} were collectively designated as a National Historic Site under the name "ЕҢyamato Kofun Group."<ref>{{еӣҪжҢҮе®ҡж–ҮеҢ–иІЎзӯүгғҮгғјгӮҝгғҷгғјгӮ№|401|00003861|еӨ§е’ҢеҸӨеўізҫӨ|1=ЕҢyamato Kofun Group}}</ref>
== Main Kofun ==
The largest tomb in the ЕҢyamato Kofun Group is the aforementioned {{ill|Nishitonozuka Kofun|ja|иҘҝж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishitonozuka Kofun|qq=}}, classified as a "great king tomb." Currently designated as the burial site of {{ill|Princess Tashiraka|ja|жүӢзҷҪйҰҷзҡҮеҘі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Tashiraka|qq=}}, it is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|е®®еҶ…еәҒ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Agency|qq=}}, restricting public and academic access without special permission. This designation was made in {{ill|1876|ja|жҳҺжІ»9е№ҙ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}}, although Te-shirakano-hime (consort of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|з¶ҷдҪ“еӨ©зҡҮ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Keitai|qq=}}) lived during the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/6th century|qq=}}. However, the estimated construction period of the tomb dates back to the late {{ill|3rd century|ja|3дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd century|qq=}} to the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4дё–зҙҖ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/4th century|qq=}}, suggesting it served as a royal tomb during the early {{ill|Yamato Kingship|ja|гғӨгғһгғҲзҺӢжЁ©|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kingship|qq=}}.
Nearby, the {{ill|Higashitonozuka Kofun|ja|жқұж®ҝеЎҡеҸӨеўі|1=Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonozuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashitonotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (175 meters) belongs to the KayЕҚ subgroup and is thought to have been constructed in the early 4th century.
Other significant kofun include seven keyhole-shaped kofun measuring 100вҖ“140 meters and three keyhole-shaped tombs with rectangular backs.
The {{ill|Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|ja|дёӯеұұеӨ§еЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama ЕҢtsuka Kofun|qq=}} (120 meters) contains fragments of {{ill|special pedestal-shaped vessels|ja|зү№ж®ҠеҷЁеҸ°|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special pedestal-shaped vessels|qq=}}, indicating a possible origin in the early Kofun period, as noted in 1986. In 1993, a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=з«Әз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|4=zh|5=иұҺз©ҙејҸзҹіе®Ө|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} was discovered in the rear circular mound. This chamber features slab stones primarily composed of pyroxene andesite (identified as {{ill|Sanukite|ja|гӮөгғҢгӮ«гӮӨгғҲ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanukite|qq=}}).
The {{ill|Nishiyama Kofun|ja|иҘҝеұұеЎҡеҸӨеўі|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Kofun|qq=}}, which is speculated to be the true tomb of Te-shirakano-hime, is the only tomb within this group dating to the first half of the 6th century.
